summary_text:
S:
The patient reports intermittent acid reflux since childhood, with worsening over the past few years. He describes the sensation as burning in his chest and throat. He denies difficulty swallowing or abdominal pain. He reports no nausea, vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, or rectal bleeding. He has experienced symptoms related to stress and caffeine consumption. He has not cut out spicy foods or citrus products completely. He reports a stable weight, though reduced appetite over the past two months. He has had a baby with intestinal surgery at one month old.

O:
Physical exam reveals well-healed abdominal surgical scars. Vital signs are within normal limits. Neurological exam is unremarkable.

A:
The patient's symptoms are consistent with a several-year history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). The patient's symptoms are likely exacerbated by stress and caffeine consumption. A trial of proton pump inhibitors is warranted. An upper endoscopy is recommended to evaluate for underlying causes of GERD.

P:
Rescribe nexium 40 mg twice daily. Schedule an upper endoscopy. Recommend dietary modifications to avoid spicy foods, caffeine, and citrus products. Advise the patient to follow up in one week to assess response to medication and dietary changes. Schedule a follow-up appointment to discuss endoscopy results and further management strategies.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Acid reflux.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Michelle King is a 35 y.o. female who presents to clinic today for a new patient evaluation of acid reflux. She was referred by her primary care physician, Dr. Scott.

The patient reports severe acid reflux that has increased in severity in recent years. She explains that she has struggled with acid reflux since approximately age 12 and she describes a burning sensation in her chest that travels up her esophagus. She denies any dysphagia, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding. She also denies any unintentional weight loss, although she admits to having a poor appetite due to her being under immense stress for the past 2 months. She has identified triggers for her symptoms, including stress, spicy foods, soda, and citrus or tomato-based products. She admits that she does still eat her trigger foods sometimes despite the exacerbation of her symptoms.

Ms. King has been taking omeprazole 40 mg twice daily for approximately 1 year. She believes that the use of omeprazole provides some relief of her symptoms as there are some days when she is asymptomatic. The only other medication she has tried is ranitidine which she took concurrently with omeprazole and she believes this helped. The patient denies undergoing an EGD within the last 10 years.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Anxiety.

Surgical
Surgery as an infant for “twisted intestines.”

Procedures
Possible EGD in youth, but none in the last 10 years.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Works as a paralegal.

FAMILY HISTORY

Maternal grandmother with esophageal cancer.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.

ALLERGIES

Seasonal environmental allergies.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Constitutional Symptoms:  Positive for loss of appetite.
• Gastrointestinal: Negative for constipation, diarrhea, abdominal pain, difficulty swallowing, nausea, vomiting, blood in stools, unexplained change in bowel habits. Positive for heartburn.
• Psychiatric:  Positive for stress and anxiety.
• Allergic/Immunologic:  Positive for seasonal allergies.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Gastrointestinal
There is a well-healed abdominal surgical scar.

ASSESSMENT

The patient presents today with a several year history of GERD that primarily manifests as heartburn and has been poorly controlled with omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. I suspect that she likely has GERD with underlying functional etiology as well. Reassuringly, she has no unintentional weight loss and denies any dysphagia. The patient has been able to identify triggers of her symptoms, including stress, spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods.

• GERD
• Suspected underlying functional etiology of heartburn.

PLAN

• I recommended dietary and lifestyle modifications like cutting out her known trigger foods and trying to reduce her stress levels to help with her symptoms.
• I prescribed Nexium 40 mg twice daily to replace her current omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. I sent this prescription to her pharmacy.
• I also recommended she undergo an EGD. We discussed the potential risks of an endoscopic procedure. The patient understands the risks and is agreeable with the plan. She scheduled this procedure today.
• If her EGD is normal and her symptoms persist despite a trial of Nexium, we can consider a trial of Protonix and obtain a pH impedance study. This will allow us to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and to decipher between a functional etiology and hypersensitivity.
• The patient was counselled to call my office if anything changes or if she has any problems before her EGD or follow-up appointment.
• She will follow up with me in clinic after her EGD to discussed results and provide an update on the Nexium.

INSTRUCTIONS

• Avoid trigger foods and try to reduce stress levels.
• Stop omeprazole 40 mg twice daily and start Nexium 40 mg twice daily instead.
• Schedule EGD.
• Call with any changes or problems.
• Schedule a follow up visit after the EGD.

sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports intermittent acid reflux since childhood, with worsening over the past few years. He describes the sensation as burning in his chest and throat. He denies difficulty swallowing or abdominal pain. He reports no nausea, vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, or rectal bleeding. He has experienced symptoms related to stress and caffeine consumption. He has not cut out spicy foods or citrus products completely. He reports a stable weight, though reduced appetite over the past two months. sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Physical exam reveals well-healed abdominal surgical scars. Vital signs are within normal limits. Neurological exam is unremarkable.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient's symptoms are consistent with a several-year history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). The patient's symptoms are likely exacerbated by stress and caffeine consumption. A trial of proton pump inhibitors is warranted. An upper endoscopy is recommended to evaluate for underlying causes of GERD.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: Rescribe nexium 40 mg twice daily. Schedule an upper endoscopy. Recommend dietary modifications to avoid spicy foods, caffeine, and citrus products. Advise the patient to follow up in one week to assess response to medication and dietary changes. Schedule a follow-up appointment to discuss endoscopy results and further management strategies.

section_miscategorizations.text: He has had a baby with intestinal surgery at one month old.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a past surgical history detail (objective medical history) rather than a current subjective symptom or concern; however, it does not substantially mislead the clinical meaning.

section_miscategorizations.text: Physical exam reveals well-healed abdominal surgical scars.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The presence of abdominal surgical scars implies a prior surgery that is not otherwise documented; this would more cleanly belong in past history, but its placement in O as an exam finding is acceptable and only a minor organizational issue.

section_miscategorizations.text: A trial of proton pump inhibitors is warranted.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a management decision (treatment plan) rather than diagnostic assessment; it should be documented as part of the plan, not the assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: An upper endoscopy is recommended to evaluate for underlying causes of GERD.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is an order/recommendation for a diagnostic procedure and belongs in the plan section, not in the assessment.
The wording suggests a continuation or renewal of an existing medication, which is appropriate in P as a treatment change/order; no true miscategorization, but the intent could be clearer.
